title: Acromioclavicular joint: anatomy and dislocation
body_html:
Key Points
  • Joint allows pectoral movement
  • Synovial joint
  • Dislocation is a common injury presenting to A&E departments
  • Typically from fall onto shoulder or direct blow
  • Severity classified using Rockwood system and graded from 1-6
  • Grades I-III usually treated conservatively

Image sourced from Wikipedia
Illustration of intact shoulder ligaments

Rockwood classification of AC dislocation

GradeAC ligamentCC ligamentJoint capsuledeltoid and trapezius musclesSpecific features
1SprainIn tactIn tactIn tactclavicle not elevated relative to acromion
2RupturedSprainRupturedin tactclavicle elevated but not above the superior border of the acromion
3RupturedRupturedRuptureddetachedclavicle elevated above the superior border of the acromion but coracoclavicular distance is less than twice normal (i.e. <25 mm)
4RupturedRupturedRuptureddetachedclavicle displaced posteriorly into trapezius
5RupturedRupturedRuptureddetachedclavicle is markedly elevated and coracoclavicular distance is more than double normal (i.e. >25 mm)
6RupturedRupturedRuptureddetachedclavicle inferiorly displaced behind coracobrachialis and biceps tendons

Video illustrating severity of AC dislocation

6
